lines.line: # Introduction to the Oral History Interview

> The speaker introduces herself as Megan Gilbert, a student conducting an oral history interview with Mark Pemble in collaboration with the College of Idaho and the Idaho State Historical Society. The interview aims to explore Mark's experiences as an active community member in Caldwell, Idaho, and his perspective on the region's development.

metadata.title: Introduction to the Oral History Interview
metadata.synopsis: The speaker introduces herself as Megan Gilbert, a student conducting an oral history interview with Mark Pemble in collaboration with the College of Idaho and the Idaho State Historical Society. The interview aims to explore Mark's experiences as an active community member in Caldwell, Idaho, and his perspective on the region's development.

lines.line: # Mark's Background and Early Work

> The speaker, Mark Pemble, shares that he has lived in the Treasure Valley for his entire life, with brief stays in California and Montana. He recounts his early work experiences, including delivering newspapers and working at Albertsons, emphasizing the importance of being able to walk or bike to work.

metadata.title: Mark's Background and Early Work
metadata.synopsis: The speaker, Mark Pemble, shares that he has lived in the Treasure Valley for his entire life, with brief stays in California and Montana. He recounts his early work experiences, including delivering newspapers and working at Albertsons, emphasizing the importance of being able to walk or bike to work.

lines.line: # Childhood Neighborhoods and Community

> Mark describes the neighborhoods he grew up in, from a diverse community in Nampa to a more upscale area in Caldwell. He reflects on the sense of community he experienced in different spaces, such as parks and churches, during his childhood.

metadata.title: Childhood Neighborhoods and Community
metadata.synopsis: Mark describes the neighborhoods he grew up in, from a diverse community in Nampa to a more upscale area in Caldwell. He reflects on the sense of community he experienced in different spaces, such as parks and churches, during his childhood.

lines.line: # Skateboarding and Its Influence on Community Perception

> The speaker discusses how skateboarding influenced his perception of community, describing it as a meritocracy where skill level determined acceptance. He also talks about the skateboarding scene in his youth and its role in forming friendships and a sense of place.

metadata.title: Skateboarding and Its Influence on Community Perception
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses how skateboarding influenced his perception of community, describing it as a meritocracy where skill level determined acceptance. He also talks about the skateboarding scene in his youth and its role in forming friendships and a sense of place.

lines.line: # Urban Planning and Biking Advocacy

> Mark's passion for skateboarding led him to become involved in urban planning, particularly in designing Caldwell's second skate park. He discusses his role in advocating for bike paths and pedestrian-friendly infrastructure in the city.

metadata.title: Urban Planning and Biking Advocacy
metadata.synopsis: Mark's passion for skateboarding led him to become involved in urban planning, particularly in designing Caldwell's second skate park. He discusses his role in advocating for bike paths and pedestrian-friendly infrastructure in the city.

lines.line: # Biking Culture in the Treasure Valley

> The speaker describes the biking scene in the Treasure Valley, noting that while there are pockets of strong bike culture, the overall community is not as engaged in biking. He shares his personal experience of biking from Caldwell to Lucky Peak to demonstrate the valley's bikeability.

metadata.title: Biking Culture in the Treasure Valley
metadata.synopsis: The speaker describes the biking scene in the Treasure Valley, noting that while there are pockets of strong bike culture, the overall community is not as engaged in biking. He shares his personal experience of biking from Caldwell to Lucky Peak to demonstrate the valley's bikeability.

lines.line: # Mark's Community Group Involvement

> Mark details his involvement in various community groups, including the cable tram depot, friends of the library, and the Caldwell Young Professionals. He emphasizes the importance of these groups in creating a sense of belonging and interconnectedness within the community.

metadata.title: Mark's Community Group Involvement
metadata.synopsis: Mark details his involvement in various community groups, including the cable tram depot, friends of the library, and the Caldwell Young Professionals. He emphasizes the importance of these groups in creating a sense of belonging and interconnectedness within the community.

lines.line: # The Caldwell Train Depot's Historical and Current Role

> The Caldwell train depot, once a vital transportation hub, now serves as a museum and event space. Mark discusses the depot's history, its transition from a train station to a community asset, and his role in the depot's committee.

metadata.title: The Caldwell Train Depot's Historical and Current Role
metadata.synopsis: The Caldwell train depot, once a vital transportation hub, now serves as a museum and event space. Mark discusses the depot's history, its transition from a train station to a community asset, and his role in the depot's committee.

lines.line: # The Evolving Role of Libraries

> Mark talks about the Friends of the Library organization and its function as a tax loophole to support library programs. He reflects on the changing role of libraries from quiet spaces for books to vibrant community hubs for various activities and groups.

metadata.title: The Evolving Role of Libraries
metadata.synopsis: Mark talks about the Friends of the Library organization and its function as a tax loophole to support library programs. He reflects on the changing role of libraries from quiet spaces for books to vibrant community hubs for various activities and groups.

lines.line: # Development of Third Spaces in Caldwell

> The speaker reflects on the development of third spaces in Caldwell, such as the Flying M coffeehouse and Indian Creek Plaza, and their impact on community life. He notes that while these spaces have improved community engagement, Caldwell still has room to grow to match its population size.

metadata.title: Development of Third Spaces in Caldwell
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reflects on the development of third spaces in Caldwell, such as the Flying M coffeehouse and Indian Creek Plaza, and their impact on community life. He notes that while these spaces have improved community engagement, Caldwell still has room to grow to match its population size.

lines.line: # Housing and Community Identity in Caldwell

> Mark discusses how housing affordability has historically attracted people to Caldwell, but emphasizes that the city's identity and the desire for a quieter lifestyle are stronger factors in why people choose to live there. He also touches on the perception of Caldwell within the broader Treasure Valley.

metadata.title: Housing and Community Identity in Caldwell
metadata.synopsis: Mark discusses how housing affordability has historically attracted people to Caldwell, but emphasizes that the city's identity and the desire for a quieter lifestyle are stronger factors in why people choose to live there. He also touches on the perception of Caldwell within the broader Treasure Valley.

lines.line: # Treasure Valley's Collective Identity

> The speaker discusses the collective identity of the Treasure Valley, noting that while older residents may feel more connected to individual cities, younger people see the valley as a unified community. He also addresses the stigma and soft racism that exists between Boise and surrounding areas.

metadata.title: Treasure Valley's Collective Identity
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the collective identity of the Treasure Valley, noting that while older residents may feel more connected to individual cities, younger people see the valley as a unified community. He also addresses the stigma and soft racism that exists between Boise and surrounding areas.

lines.line: # Personal Identity as an Idahoan

> Mark contemplates the broad and diverse nature of being an Idahoan, expressing difficulty in labeling himself within traditional perceptions of Idaho identity. He feels connected to both the local community and the wider region of the northwest.

metadata.title: Personal Identity as an Idahoan
metadata.synopsis: Mark contemplates the broad and diverse nature of being an Idahoan, expressing difficulty in labeling himself within traditional perceptions of Idaho identity. He feels connected to both the local community and the wider region of the northwest.

lines.line: # Significant Changes and Nostalgia

> When asked about changes in the Treasure Valley, Mark expresses nostalgia for the interurban trolley system, despite never experiencing it. He believes that the region has improved over time, with more job variety and a welcoming atmosphere.

metadata.title: Significant Changes and Nostalgia
metadata.synopsis: When asked about changes in the Treasure Valley, Mark expresses nostalgia for the interurban trolley system, despite never experiencing it. He believes that the region has improved over time, with more job variety and a welcoming atmosphere.

lines.line: # Vision for the Future of the Treasure Valley

> Mark shares his vision for the future of the Treasure Valley, predicting that the population will eventually exceed one million. He emphasizes the importance of sustainability and self-sufficiency, particularly in light of the valley's isolation and the challenges of resource transportation.

metadata.title: Vision for the Future of the Treasure Valley
metadata.synopsis: Mark shares his vision for the future of the Treasure Valley, predicting that the population will eventually exceed one million. He emphasizes the importance of sustainability and self-sufficiency, particularly in light of the valley's isolation and the challenges of resource transportation.

children.text: # Introduction to the Oral History Interview

> The speaker introduces herself as Megan Gilbert, a student conducting an oral history interview with Mark Pemble in collaboration with the College of Idaho and the Idaho State Historical Society. The interview aims to explore Mark's experiences as an active community member in Caldwell, Idaho, and his perspective on the region's development.
# Mark's Background and Early Work

> The speaker, Mark Pemble, shares that he has lived in the Treasure Valley for his entire life, with brief stays in California and Montana. He recounts his early work experiences, including delivering newspapers and working at Albertsons, emphasizing the importance of being able to walk or bike to work.
# Childhood Neighborhoods and Community

> Mark describes the neighborhoods he grew up in, from a diverse community in Nampa to a more upscale area in Caldwell. He reflects on the sense of community he experienced in different spaces, such as parks and churches, during his childhood.
# Skateboarding and Its Influence on Community Perception

> The speaker discusses how skateboarding influenced his perception of community, describing it as a meritocracy where skill level determined acceptance. He also talks about the skateboarding scene in his youth and its role in forming friendships and a sense of place.
# Urban Planning and Biking Advocacy

> Mark's passion for skateboarding led him to become involved in urban planning, particularly in designing Caldwell's second skate park. He discusses his role in advocating for bike paths and pedestrian-friendly infrastructure in the city.
# Biking Culture in the Treasure Valley

> The speaker describes the biking scene in the Treasure Valley, noting that while there are pockets of strong bike culture, the overall community is not as engaged in biking. He shares his personal experience of biking from Caldwell to Lucky Peak to demonstrate the valley's bikeability.
# Mark's Community Group Involvement

> Mark details his involvement in various community groups, including the cable tram depot, friends of the library, and the Caldwell Young Professionals. He emphasizes the importance of these groups in creating a sense of belonging and interconnectedness within the community.
# The Caldwell Train Depot's Historical and Current Role

> The Caldwell train depot, once a vital transportation hub, now serves as a museum and event space. Mark discusses the depot's history, its transition from a train station to a community asset, and his role in the depot's committee.
# The Evolving Role of Libraries

> Mark talks about the Friends of the Library organization and its function as a tax loophole to support library programs. He reflects on the changing role of libraries from quiet spaces for books to vibrant community hubs for various activities and groups.
# Development of Third Spaces in Caldwell

> The speaker reflects on the development of third spaces in Caldwell, such as the Flying M coffeehouse and Indian Creek Plaza, and their impact on community life. He notes that while these spaces have improved community engagement, Caldwell still has room to grow to match its population size.
# Housing and Community Identity in Caldwell

> Mark discusses how housing affordability has historically attracted people to Caldwell, but emphasizes that the city's identity and the desire for a quieter lifestyle are stronger factors in why people choose to live there. He also touches on the perception of Caldwell within the broader Treasure Valley.
# Treasure Valley's Collective Identity

> The speaker discusses the collective identity of the Treasure Valley, noting that while older residents may feel more connected to individual cities, younger people see the valley as a unified community. He also addresses the stigma and soft racism that exists between Boise and surrounding areas.
# Personal Identity as an Idahoan

> Mark contemplates the broad and diverse nature of being an Idahoan, expressing difficulty in labeling himself within traditional perceptions of Idaho identity. He feels connected to both the local community and the wider region of the northwest.
# Significant Changes and Nostalgia

> When asked about changes in the Treasure Valley, Mark expresses nostalgia for the interurban trolley system, despite never experiencing it. He believes that the region has improved over time, with more job variety and a welcoming atmosphere.
# Vision for the Future of the Treasure Valley

> Mark shares his vision for the future of the Treasure Valley, predicting that the population will eventually exceed one million. He emphasizes the importance of sustainability and self-sufficiency, particularly in light of the valley's isolation and the challenges of resource transportation.

story.description: The interview, conducted by Megan Gilbert, focuses on Mark Pemble's experiences as an active community member in Caldwell, Idaho. Mark shares insights on the changing dynamics of Caldwell and the broader Treasure Valley, touching on urban planning, community development, and the interplay of local identity versus regional identity. He elaborates on his involvement in initiatives like the creation of skate parks and bike paths, underscoring a commitment to creating accessible community spaces. The interview also covers sociocultural changes in Caldwell, such as demographic shifts and the role of museums and libraries as community hubs. Mark emphasizes the importance of integrated growth, the challenge of addressing infrastructural needs, and his hope for a future of sustainable development within the Treasure Valley.
story.title: Pemble_Mark_OH2817 Hist305
